title: AQUILA: Communication Efficient Federated Learning with Adaptive Quantization of Lazily-Aggregated Gradients

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - AQUILA: COMMUNICATION EFFICIENT FEDER ATED LEARNING WITH ADAPTIVE QUANTIZATION OF LAZILY-AGGREGATED GRADIENTS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

The development and deployment of federated learning (FL) have been bottlenecked by the heavy communication overheads of high-dimensional models between the distributed client nodes and the central server. To achieve better errorcommunication tradeoffs, recent efforts have been made to either adaptively reduce the communication frequency by skipping unimportant updates, a.k.a. lazilyaggregated quantization (LAQ), or adjust the quantization bits for each communication. In this paper, we propose a unifying communication efficient framework for FL based on **a** daptive **qu** ant **i** zation of **l** azily- **a** ggregated gradients ( **AQUILA** ), which adaptively adjusts two mutually-dependent factors, the communication frequency and the quantization level, in a synergistic way. Specifically, we start from a careful investigation on the classical LAQ scheme and formulate AQUILA as an optimization problem where the optimal quantization level per communication is selected by minimizing the gradient loss caused by updates skipping. Meanwhile, we adjust the LAQ strategy to better fit the novel quantization criterion and thus keep the communication frequency at an appropriate level. The effectiveness and convergence of the proposed AQUILA framework are theoretically verified. The experimental results demonstrate that AQUILA can reduce around **50%** of overall transmitted bits compared to existing methods while achieving the same level of model accuracy in a number of non-homogeneous FL scenarios, including Non-IID data distribution and heterogeneous model architecture. The proposed AQUILA is highly adaptive and compatible to existing FL settings.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

With the deployment of ubiquitous sensing and computing devices, the Internet of things (IoT) as well as many other distributed systems have gradually grown from concept to reality, bringing dramatic convenience to people’s daily life (Du et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020a; Hard et al., 2018). To fully utilize such distributed computing resources, distributed learning provides a promising framework that can achieve comparable performance with the traditional centralized learning scheme. However, the privacy and security of sensitive data during the updating and transmitting processes in distributed learning have been a growing concern. In this context, _federated learning (FL)_ (McMahan et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2019) has been developed, enabling distributed devices to collaboratively learn a global model without privacy leakage by keeping private data sets isolated and masking transmitted information with secure approaches like differential privacy (Abadi et al., 2016), secret sharing techniques (Bonawitz et al., 2017) and homomorphic encryption (Liu et al., 2020b). Due to its privacy and security preserving property and great potentials in some distributed but privacysensitive fields like finance and health, FL has attracted tremendous attentions from both academia and industry in recent years.

Unfortunately, in many FL applications like image classification and objective recognition, the models to be trained tend to be high-dimensional, which lead to heavy communication overheads, for example, a Resnet-152 network has over 58 million parameters (He et al., 2016). Hence, communication efficiency has become one of the key bottlenecks of FL. To this end, recent researches have tried to reduce the communication frequency, for example, Sun et al. (2020) proposes the lazily-aggregated quantization (LAQ) method to reduce communication rounds by skipping some

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

unnecessary parameter uploads. To further reduce transmitted bits per communication, LAQ can be used jointly with gradient compression techniques, e.g. quantization and sparsification (Strom, 2015; Wangni et al., 2018; Lin et al., 2018; Han et al., 2020). Moreover, Mao et al. (2021) develops the Adaptive Quantized Gradient (AQG) for LAQ to adjust the quantization bit among multiple given levels during training. However, the AQG is not sufficiently adaptive, for example, in the two-level AQG with 4 bit and 2 bit, the situation of 3 bit and 1 bit is not covered at all. In a separate line of work, Jhunjhunwala et al. (2021) develops an adaptive quantization rule (AdaQuantFL) for FL which can search in a given range for an optimal quantization level and achieve a better error-communication tradeoff.

Previous work has investigated how to optimize communication frequency or adjust quantization levels in a highly adaptive fashion, but not both. Intuitively, we ask the question, _can we adaptively adjust the quantization level in LAQ to further reduce communication rounds and transmitted bits simultaneously?_ A straightforward approach is to train LAQ jointly with state-of-the-art adaptive quantization methods like AdaQuantFL. However, quantization level and communication frequency are mutually dependent in establishing the model convergence and must be adjusted together. For example, lazy aggregation leads to the skip of some gradients that no longer require quantization, while the choice of quantization levels directly affects the quality of a gradient update and thus whether it is selected for transmission and aggregation. Therefore, the key question of our work is how to jointly leverage these two complementary yet mutually-dependent degrees of freedom for further optimizing communication efficiency in FL.

The key idea of this paper is to select the optimal quantization bits for each communication round in LAQ by optimizing the gradient loss caused by skipping quantized updates, which gives a novel quantization criterion that can cooperate with LAQ strategy to further reduce overall transmitted bits while maintaining the desired convergence properties of LAQ. The contributions of this paper are trifold and summarized as follows. 1) We propose a FL framework with **a** daptive **qu** ant **i** zation of **l** azily- **a** ggregated gradients termed AQUILA, which simultaneously adjusts the communication frequency and quantization level in a synergistic fashion. 2) We formulate AQUILA as an optimization problem and develop an upperbound for the gradient loss caused by communication skipping, which gives a novel adaptive quantization criterion which is theoretically proven to be more efficient compared to AdaQuantFL while maintaining the same convergence properties. 3) We experimentally evaluate the performance of AQUILA in a number of non-homogeneous FL settings, including Non-IID data distribution and various heterogeneous model architecture. The experimental results show that AQUILA can significantly mitigate the communication overhead over a number of baselines including fixed-bit LAQ and the naive combination of LAQ and AdaQuantFL. Our approach is highly adaptive and compatible to existing FL settings.

Recently, AdaQuantFL is proposed to achieve a better error-communication tradeoff by adaptively adjusting the quantization levels during FL training (Jhunjhunwala et al., 2021). Specifically, AdaQuantFL computes iteration _k_ ’s optimal quantization level _b[∗] k_[based on the following criterion] involving training loss and initial quantization level _b_ 0:
where _f_ ( _**θ**_[0] ) and _f_ ( _**θ**[k]_ ) are the global training loss at iteration 0 and _k_ , respectively.

However, AdaQuantFL transmits quantized gradients at **every iteration** . In order to skip unnecessary communication rounds and adaptively adjust quantization level for each communication jointly, an naive approach is to quantize lazily aggregated gradients with AdaQuantFL. Nevertheless, it fails to achieve efficient communication due to a number of reasons. Firstly, given the descending trend of training loss, AdaQuantFL’s criterion (6) may lead to high quantization bit number even exceeding 32 late in the training process, which is too much for cases where the global convergence is already approaching. Secondly, higher quantization level results in smaller quantization error, which will lead to lower communication threshold in LAQ’s criterion (4) and thus higher frequency of transmission. Therefore it is desirable to develop more efficient adaptive quantization method in the lazily-aggregated setting to systematically improve communication efficiency in FL.

## 3 METHOD

## 3.1 ADAPTIVE QUANTIZATION OF LAZILY-AGGREGATED GRADIENTS (AQUILA)

Given the above limitations of the naive joint use of existing adaptive quantization criterion and lazy aggregation strategy, this paper aims to design a unifying framework for communication efficiency optimization where the quantization level and communication frequency are adjusted in a synergistic and interactive way. Based on a careful investigation on LAQ, we design a novel quantization criterion where the optimal quantization level is selected by minimizing the expected gradient loss caused by skipping quantized updates. The rationale behind such strategy is, by formulating the adaptive quantization problem of lazily-aggregated gradients as optimizing the expected gradient loss with respect to the number of quantization bits, we can get an adaptive quantization criterion based on local gradient updates while maintaining or even improving the convergence property of

The superiority of (7) comes from the following two aspects. Firstly, the gradient updates tend to fluctuate along with the training process instead of keeping descending like the loss value, and thus prevent the quantization level from increasing tremendously compared with the initial level. Secondly, with lazy aggregation criterion based on gradient updates like (4), the transmitted bits in AQUILA is further controlled, since the gradient update for actual transmission in (7) is lower bounded by the lazy aggregation criterion, and therefore high-bit transmission for small update is more likely to be skipped.

Figure 1: The schematic illustration of the communication efficient FL with AQUILA in comparison with the naive combination of AdaQuantFL and LAQ. The blue lines indicating the transmission of quantized gradients in AQUILA are drawn in different thicknesses to represent different quantization levels selected by various clients.

The cooperation of the novel adaptive quantization criterion (7) and the modified lazy aggregation strategy (8) is illustrated in Fig. 1a. Compared to the naive combination of AdaQuantFL and LAQ where the mutual influence between adaptive quantization and lazy aggregation has not been considered as shown in Fig. 1b, our AQUILA framework adaptively optimizes the allocation of quantization bits throughout training to promote the convergence of lazy aggregation, and at the same time utilizes the lazy aggregation strategy to improve the efficiency of adaptive quantization by skipping high-bit transmission. The proposed AQUILA’s effect of suppressing the transmission of high quantization bits has been verified in our experiments, as shown in Fig. 20 in the Appendix. Besides, with the adjusted lazy aggregation strategy (8), AQUILA well addresses the problem of high communication frequency in the late training process of naive combination of AdaQuantFL and LAQ, as indicated by Fig. 2.

The proposed AQUILA is summarized as follows in Algorithm 1. At iteration _k_ = 0, all clients are forced to transmit local gradients quantized with the initial level _b_ 0. At iteration _k ∈{_ 1 _,_ 2 _, ..., K}_ , the server first broadcasts the global model _**θ**[k]_ to all clients. Each client _m_ computes _**g**[k] m_[with] local training data, and then uses it to select an optimal quantization level by (7). Then, each client

## 3.2 THEORETICAL DERIVATION AND ANALYSIS OF AQUILA

## 4 EXPERIMENTS AND DISCUSSION

## 4.1 EXPERIMENT SETUP

**Dataset.** In this paper, we evaluate our method with MNIST and CIFAR10 dataset, considering both IID and Non-IID data distribution. To simulate Non-IID situation, each client is assigned with two classes of data at most and the amount of data for each class is balanced.

**Parameters.** We set total client number _M_ = 10, and follow the settings in LAQ, where _D_ = 10 and _ξ_ 1 = _ξ_ 2 = _· · ·_ = _ξD_ = 0 _._ 8 _/D_ . In terms of initial quantization level, a low level _b_ 0 = 2 is chosen for simpler tasks with MNIST, and a larger level _b_ 0 = 6 is selected for more complex tasks with CIFAR10. For both AdaQuantFL and AQUILA, we set an upperbound for quantization level as _bmax_ = 16 in case that the level grows too high.
**Training.** We train a CNN with MNIST and a Resnet18 network with CIFAR10. The hyperparameters of our experiments are shown in Table 1 in Appendix.

We first evaluate our proposed AQUILA with homogeneous settings where all the local models share the same architecture as the global model. The performance of AQUILA is compared with several state-of-the-art methods including FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017), QSGD (Alistarh et al., 2017), AdaQuantFL, fixed-bit LAQ and the naive combination of AdaQuantFL with LAQ. For the choice of quantization level for fixed-bit LAQ, since AQUILA’s initial level _b_ 0 is 2 for MNIST and 6 for CIFAR10, we compare AQUILA with LAQ-2 and LAQ-6 for MNIST and CIFAR 10 respectively. Besides, the performance of LAQ with the upperbound _bmax_ = 16 is also evaluated.

We also evaluate our proposed AQUILA with HeteroFL (Diao et al., 2020), where the local models trained at clients’ side are heterogeneous. ## 4.2 PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS

In this part, we analyze the performance of AQUILA with various experiment settings including Non-IID data distribution and heterogeneous model architecture. From figures in this paper, we can observe that:

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

- AQUILA achieves a significant transmission reduction on both MNIST and CIFAR10 as compared to AdaQuantFL and the naive combination of LAQ and AdaQuantFL. For instance, AQUILA saves 65.98% of transmitted bits compared with AdaQuantFL and 62.05% compared with the naive combination of LAQ and AdaQuantFL in the IID scenario with CIFAR10 dataset as shown in Fig. 3d, and other figures all show an obvious reduction in terms of the total transmitted bits required for convergence. Fig. 4 verifies that the quantization level selected by AQUILA will not continuously increase during training like AdaQuantFL and LAQ with AdaQuantFL.

- Similarly, AQUILA outperforms all fixed-level LAQ in terms of overall transmitted bits for both MNIST and CIFAR10, as shown in Fig. 3. For example, Fig. 3a indicates that AQUILA reduces 42.6% of total transmitted bits compared with LAQ-2 and 92.32% compared with LAQ-16 in MNIST. The transmission reduction is 38.76% for LAQ-6 and 78.09% for LAQ-16 in CIFAR10 as shown in Fig. 3e. Besides, Fig. 3 and Fig. 4 indicate that although LAQ with fixed but low quantization level like LAQ-2 and LAQ-4 sometimes transmit smaller amount of bits per step compared with AQUILA, they suffer from lower accuracy and slower convergence. It further verifies the necessity and effectiveness of our well-designed adaptive quantization criterion which achieves fast convergence with similar low-bit transmission but without degradation of the model performance.

- AQUILA also works well with heterogeneous local models. With the two various ways of distributing the heterogeneous local models in this paper, our proposed AQUILA still outperforms other methods by significantly reducing overall transmitted bits while maintaining the same convergence property and model accuracy. Please refer to Fig. 10 to Fig. 19 in the Appendix for more detailed information. These experimental results in nonhomogeneous FL settings prove that our proposed AQUILA can be used in a more general and complicated federated learning scenarios.

## 5 CONCLUSIONS AND FUTURE WORK

This paper proposes a communication efficient FL framework to simultaneously adjust two mutually-dependent degrees of freedom: communication frequency and quantization level. With the close cooperation of the novel adaptive quantization and adjusted lazy aggregation strategy developed in this paper, the proposed AQUILA has been proven to be capable of reducing the transmitted bits while maintaining the same convergence property and model performance compared against existing methods both theoretically and experimentally. The evaluation with Non-IID data distribution and various heterogeneous model architectures demonstrates that AQUILA is compatible to existing FL settings. Future works include further improvements and theoretical guarantee for FL systems with heterogeneity.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes AQUILA, a federated learning framework that jointly optimizes communication frequency and quantization levels. While the approach appears promising, there are significant concerns about the theoretical analysis, experimental evaluation, and methodological rigor that need to be addressed.

The paper presents an interesting idea of jointly optimizing two communication efficiency levers in federated learning. The motivation is clear and builds logically on prior work like LAQ and AdaQuantFL. However, the theoretical derivation contains questionable steps, the experimental evaluation is limited in scope, and key assumptions about the federated setting are not adequately justified.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical Analysis Gap Between Theorem 1 and Adaptive Criterion: The derivation from Theorem 1's bound (Equation 9) to the adaptive quantization criterion (Equation 7) contains a critical logical gap. Equation 10 shows the optimal bits from minimizing the bound, but the paper then claims Equation 7 follows by dividing by b_m^1. This step assumes the ratio of unknown parameters (σ², d_m, B_m) remains constant across iterations, which is not justified. Specifically, B_m (total bits sent) clearly changes over time, making this division invalid. The theoretical foundation for the main adaptive criterion is therefore incomplete.

- Unrealistic Assumptions in Convergence Analysis: The convergence analysis relies on Assumption 2 which states quantization is unbiased with variance bounded by q_b||w||². However, this assumption is violated by the adaptive quantization scheme itself. When clients use different quantization levels b_m^k across iterations, the unbiasedness property no longer holds globally. The analysis also assumes smooth loss functions (Assumption 1) but doesn't address how this interacts with the gradient skipping mechanism, which can introduce non-smooth behavior due to threshold effects.

- Limited Experimental Evaluation on Non-IID Data: The paper claims evaluation on non-IID data but only uses a mild form of heterogeneity (2 classes per client). Real-world federated learning often involves much more extreme non-IID distributions where some clients may have only a single class or highly imbalanced data. The experiments don't test AQUILA's robustness under these challenging conditions. Moreover, the paper doesn't report per-client performance metrics, which are crucial for understanding whether the method helps or hurts certain clients in non-IID settings.

- Missing Scalability Analysis: The experiments only use M=10 clients, which is insufficient to demonstrate scalability. Federated learning systems often involve hundreds or thousands of clients. The lazy aggregation mechanism's behavior, particularly the threshold calculations involving sums over all clients, may not scale well. The paper lacks analysis of how communication overhead and convergence speed change as the number of clients increases.

- Inadequate Comparison to State-of-the-Art: The experimental comparisons miss several important recent baselines. Notably absent are comparisons with FedProx (which handles non-IID data), SCAFFOLD (which addresses client drift), and other recent quantization methods like FedQVR. The paper only compares against FedAvg, QSGD, AdaQuantFL, and LAQ variants. Without comparison to these more sophisticated methods, it's unclear whether AQUILA's improvements hold against the current state-of-the-art.

- Insufficient Ablation Study: The paper doesn't isolate the contribution of each component. It's unclear how much of the improvement comes from adaptive quantization versus the modified lazy aggregation criterion. A proper ablation should compare: (1) LAQ with adaptive quantization but original threshold, (2) LAQ with fixed quantization but modified threshold, and (3) the full AQUILA method. Without this, we cannot assess the relative importance of each innovation.

- Missing demonstration of quantization criterion efficiency: The paper claims AQUILA's adaptive quantization criterion (Equation 7) is more efficient than AdaQuantFL but provides no concrete demonstration. To establish this claim, the paper should compute the optimal bits for both methods on a simple, fully-specified model (e.g., logistic regression on synthetic data with known gradients) and show that AQUILA achieves lower communication cost for the same gradient approximation error. This would verify the theoretical advantage claimed in Theorem 2 and help readers understand the mechanism behind the efficiency gain.

- No worked example of gradient loss bound: Theorem 1 provides an upper bound on gradient loss, but the paper never computes this bound for a concrete case. A worked example would strengthen the theoretical contribution—for instance, calculating the bound for a quadratic loss function with known gradient statistics and showing how it varies with quantization level. This would demonstrate that the bound is non-vacuous and help calibrate intuition about how the bound translates to practical communication savings.

- Missing implementation guidance for quantization adaptation: The paper proposes an adaptive quantization mechanism but provides no practical guidance on implementation challenges. Specifically, it doesn't discuss how to handle the computational overhead of computing the optimal quantization level (Equation 7) on resource-constrained edge devices, nor how to store and track the last uploaded gradient hat{g}_m^{k-1} efficiently. A discussion of these practical considerations, including memory requirements and computational complexity, would be essential for real-world deployment.

- Unverified recovery of existing methods as special cases: The paper claims AQUILA generalizes existing approaches but never formally verifies this. It should show that with specific parameter settings, AQUILA reduces to (a) standard LAQ when quantization is fixed, and (b) AdaQuantFL when communication skipping is disabled. Demonstrating these recoveries would confirm the unifying nature of the framework and help position the contribution relative to prior work.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Fix the theoretical derivation by properly justifying the transition from Equation 10 to Equation 7, or revise the adaptive criterion to follow directly from the bound

- Expand experiments to include more extreme non-IID settings and report per-client performance metrics

- Add scalability experiments with larger numbers of clients (e.g., 50, 100, 500)

- Include comparisons with additional baselines like FedProx, SCAFFOLD, and FedQVR

- Conduct proper ablation studies to isolate the contributions of adaptive quantization versus modified lazy aggregation

comments.title: Invalid derivation from optimal bits to adaptive criterion
comments.quote:
For each client $m$, the optimal $b_{m}^{k}$ is selected by setting the first derivative of the upper bound (9) to zero:

$(b_{m}^{k})^{*}=\sqrt{\left(2\sigma^{2}B_{m}\log_{e}(2)\right)/\left(d_{m}\left\lVert\bm{g}_{m}^{k}-\hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{k-1}\right\rVert_{2}^{2}\right)}.$ (10)

Therefore, an adaptive quantization criterion which does not rely on unknown parameters such as $\sigma$ and $d_{m}$ can be achieved through dividing $b_{m}^{k}$ by $b_{m}^{1}$, which gives the adaptive selection criterion in (7) if $b_{m}^{1}$ for each client $m$ is chosen as the initial quantization level $b_{0}$.
comments.explanation: The derivation from Equation (10) to the adaptive criterion (7) is mathematically incorrect. From (10), we have $(b_m^k)^* \propto \sqrt{B_m / \|g_m^k - \hat{g}_m^{k-1}\|^2}$. Dividing by $b_m^1$ gives $(b_m^k)^*/b_m^1 \propto \sqrt{(B_m/B_1) \cdot (\|g_m^1 - \hat{g}_m^0\|^2 / \|g_m^k - \hat{g}_m^{k-1}\|^2)}$. Since $B_m$ (total bits sent) increases over time, $B_m/B_1 \neq 1$ in general. The ratio $B_m/B_1$ cannot be eliminated mathematically, making the claimed simplification to criterion (7) invalid. Rewrite the derivation to account for the $B_m/B_1$ term or provide justification for why this ratio equals 1.

comments.title: Division by zero risk in adaptive quantization criterion
comments.quote: $(b_{m}^{k})^{*}=b_{0}\cdot\sqrt{\left\|\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{1}-\boldsymbol{\hat{g}}_{m}^{0}\right\|_{2}^{2}/\left\|\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{k}-\boldsymbol{\hat{g}}_{m}^{k-1}\right\|_{2}^{2}}.$ (7)
comments.explanation: Equation (7) has a division by zero problem when $\left\|\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{k}-\boldsymbol{\hat{g}}_{m}^{k-1}\right\|_{2}^{2} = 0$, which occurs when the current gradient equals the previously uploaded quantized gradient. This would result in an undefined or infinite quantization level. Add a safeguard: $(b_{m}^{k})^{*}=b_{0}\cdot\sqrt{\left\|\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{1}-\boldsymbol{\hat{g}}_{m}^{0}\right\|_{2}^{2}/\max(\left\|\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{k}-\boldsymbol{\hat{g}}_{m}^{k-1}\right\|_{2}^{2}, \epsilon)}$ where $\epsilon > 0$ is a small constant (e.g., $10^{-8}$) to prevent division by zero.

comments.title: Undefined variables in LAQ threshold
comments.quote: $\left\lVert Q_{b}(\bm{g}_{m}^{k})-Q_{b}(\hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{k-1})\right\rVert_{2}^{2}\geq\frac{\sum_{d=1}^{D}\xi_{d}\left\lVert\bm{\theta}^{k+1-d}-\bm{\theta}^{k-d}\right\rVert_{2}^{2}}{\alpha^{2}M^{2}}+3(\left\lVert\varepsilon_{b}(\hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{k-1})\right\rVert_{2}^{2}+\left\lVert\varepsilon_{b}(\bm{g}_{m}^{k})\right\rVert_{2}^{2}),$ (4)
comments.explanation: The threshold condition is mathematically incomplete as stated. The variables $D$ and $\{\xi_{d}\}_{d=1}^{D}$ are undefined. Add definitions: let $D$ be the window size for historical parameter differences, and specify that $\{\xi_{d}\}_{d=1}^{D}$ are non-negative weights satisfying $\sum_{d=1}^{D} \xi_{d} = 1$.

comments.title: Undefined gradient notation in adaptive criterion
comments.quote: $(b_{m}^{k})^{*}=b_{0}\cdot\sqrt{\left\|\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{1}-\boldsymbol{\hat{g}}_{m}^{0}\right\|_{2}^{2}/\left\|\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{k}-\boldsymbol{\hat{g}}_{m}^{k-1}\right\|_{2}^{2}}.$ (7)
comments.explanation: The term $\boldsymbol{\hat{g}}_{m}^{0}$ in the numerator of equation (7) is not defined in the paper. While the algorithm states that at iteration $k=0$, clients transmit gradients quantized with level $b_0$, the notation for this initial quantized gradient is never explicitly introduced. This creates ambiguity in the adaptive criterion. Rewrite $(b_{m}^{k})^{*}=b_{0}\cdot\sqrt{\left\|\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{1}-\boldsymbol{\hat{g}}_{m}^{0}\right\|_{2}^{2}/\left\|\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{k}-\boldsymbol{\hat{g}}_{m}^{k-1}\right\|_{2}^{2}}.$ as $(b_{m}^{k})^{*}=b_{0}\cdot\sqrt{\left\|\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{1}-Q_{b_0}(\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{0})\right\|_{2}^{2}/\left\|\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{k}-\boldsymbol{\hat{g}}_{m}^{k-1}\right\|_{2}^{2}}.$ where $Q_{b_0}(\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{0})$ is explicitly defined as the quantized gradient at iteration 0.

comments.title: Inconsistent quantization notation in communication criterion
comments.quote: $\left\|Q_{b_{m}^{k}}(\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{k})-Q_{\hat{b}_{m}^{k-1}}(\hat{\boldsymbol{g}}_{m}^{k-1})\right\|_{2}^{2}\geq\frac{\sum_{d=1}^{D}\xi_{d}\left\|\boldsymbol{\theta}^{k+1-d}-\boldsymbol{\theta}^{k-d}\right\|_{2}^{2}}{\alpha^{2}M^{2}}+2\left\|\varepsilon_{b_{m}^{k}}(\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{k})-\varepsilon_{\hat{b}_{m}^{k-1}}(\hat{\boldsymbol{g}}_{m}^{k-1})\right\|_{2}^{2},$ (8)
comments.explanation: There is a notation inconsistency between the text description and equation (8). The text mentions "heterogeneous quantization level $b_{m}^{k}$ and $\tilde{b}_{m}^{k-1}$" but the equation uses $\hat{b}_{m}^{k-1}$. Additionally, the hat notation on $b$ is not defined anywhere in the paper. Rewrite the text to match the equation: "where all the notations are the same as in (4) except the heterogeneous quantization level $b_{m}^{k}$ and $\hat{b}_{m}^{k-1}$ for each client" and add a definition: "where $\hat{b}_{m}^{k-1}$ denotes the quantization level used for $\hat{\boldsymbol{g}}_{m}^{k-1}$ at iteration $k-1$".

comments.title: Algorithm uses undefined quantization operator
comments.quote: Calculates local gradient  $\pmb{g}_m^0$  and sends the quantized gradient  $Q_{b_0}(\pmb{g}_m^0)$ .
comments.explanation: The algorithm uses the quantization operator $Q_{b_0}(\cdot)$ but this operator is never defined in the paper. While Assumption 2 provides statistical properties of the quantization (unbiasedness and variance bound), the actual quantization scheme (e.g., uniform quantization, stochastic rounding) is not specified. Without defining $Q_b(\cdot)$, the algorithm is incomplete and cannot be implemented or analyzed properly. Add a definition of the quantization operator $Q_b(\cdot)$ before Algorithm 1.

comments.title: Algorithm references undefined condition (8)
comments.quote: if (8) holds for client  $m$  then
comments.explanation: The algorithm checks condition (8) but this condition is not defined in Algorithm 1 or the surrounding text. The paper mentions "communication criterion (8)" in the theoretical section but never provides the actual mathematical form of condition (8). This makes the algorithm's decision logic undefined and impossible to implement. Add the explicit mathematical form of condition (8) before Algorithm 1.

comments.title: Server update uses inconsistent quantization levels
comments.quote: Server updates  $\pmb{\theta}^{k + 1}$  by  $\pmb{\theta}^k -\alpha \sum_{m = 1}^{M}Q_{\hat{b}_m^k}(\hat{\pmb{g}}_m^k)$ .
comments.explanation: The server update applies quantization $Q_{\hat{b}_m^k}$ to gradients $\hat{g}_m^k$, but this creates inconsistency. When clients skip communication (lines 17-20), $\hat{g}_m^k = \hat{g}_m^{k-1}$ is the original unquantized gradient, yet the server re-quantizes it with level $\hat{b}_m^k$. This double quantization is not accounted for in the theoretical analysis, which assumes $\hat{g}_m^k$ is either the current gradient or the previously quantized gradient. Modify the server update to use $\hat{g}_m^k$ directly without re-quantization, or update the theoretical analysis to account for this double quantization.

comments.title: Theorem 1 upper bound contains undefined parameters
comments.quote:
$\mathbb{E}[\left\|\hat{\bm{\theta}}^{k+1}-\bm{\theta}^{k+1}\right\|_{2}^{2}]\leq\frac{3\alpha^{2}}{M}\sum_{m\in\mathbb{M}}\frac{d_{m}d\left\lceil\log_{2}(4b_{m}^{k})\right\rceil}{B_{m}}\left\lVert\bm{g}_{m}^{k}-\hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{k-1}\right\rVert_{2}^{2}$ (9)
$+\frac{3\alpha^{2}}{M}\sum_{m\in\mathbb{M}}\left(\frac{d\sigma^{2}}{(\hat{b}_{m}^{k-1})^{2}}+\frac{d\sigma^{2}}{(b_{m}^{k})^{2}}\right)+\frac{3\alpha^{2}}{M}\sum_{m\in\mathbb{M}}\frac{d_{m}(d+32)}{B_{m}}\left\lVert\bm{g}_{m}^{k}-\hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{k-1}\right\rVert_{2}^{2},$
comments.explanation: Theorem 1 contains parameters $d$, $d_m$, and $\sigma^2$ that are not properly defined. While $d_m$ is mentioned as "a positive constant determined by $L_m$" and $\sigma^2$ as "the maximum $\|w\|_2^2$", the parameter $d$ is completely undefined. The bound cannot be interpreted or verified without clear definitions of these parameters. Add explicit definitions for $d$, $d_m$, and $\sigma^2$ before stating Theorem 1.

comments.title: Theorem 2 inequality chain contains unjustified step
comments.quote: $(b_{m}^{k})^{*}=b_{0}\sqrt{\frac{\left\lVert\bm{g}_{m}^{1}-\hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{0}\right\rVert_{2}^{2}}{\left\lVert\bm{g}_{m}^{k}-\hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{k-1}\right\rVert_{2}^{2}}}\leq b_{0}\sqrt{\frac{\alpha^{2}M^{2}L^{3}\left\lVert\bm{\theta}^{1}-\bm{\theta}^{0}\right\rVert_{2}^{2}}{2\xi[f(\bm{\theta}^{k})-f(\bm{\theta}^{*})]}}\leq b_{0}\sqrt{\frac{f(\bm{\theta}^{0})-f(\bm{\theta}^{*})}{f(\bm{\theta}^{k})-f(\bm{\theta}^{*})}},$ (11)
comments.explanation: The second inequality in the chain replaces $\|g_m^1 - \hat{g}_m^0\|^2$ with $\alpha^2 M^2 L^3 \|\theta^1 - \theta^0\|^2/(2\xi)$, but this substitution is not justified. The gradient difference $\|g_m^1 - \hat{g}_m^0\|^2$ depends on the specific client $m$ and quantization, while the replacement term is a global bound. Without showing that $\|g_m^1 - \hat{g}_m^0\|^2 \leq \alpha^2 M^2 L^3 \|\theta^1 - \theta^0\|^2/(2\xi)$ holds for all clients, this step is mathematically invalid. Add justification for this bound or modify the inequality.

comments.title: Inconsistent quantization variable notation
comments.quote:
where $\xi_{i}(\bm{g},b)$ is a random variable defined as follows. Let $l\in\{0,1,2,...,b-1\}$ be an integer satisfying $\left|g_{i}\right|/\|\bm{g}\|_{2}\in\left[l/b,(l+1)/b\right)$, then:

\[ \xi_{i}(\bm{g},b)=\left\{\begin{array}[]{ll}(\ell+1)/b&\text{with probability }(b\cdot\|g_{i}\|)/\|\bm{g}\|_{2}-l\\
\ell/b&\text{otherwise.}\end{array}\right. \] (3)
comments.explanation:
The notation inconsistency is confirmed: the text defines integer $l$ but uses $\ell$ in the piecewise definition. The probability expression uses $\|g_{i}\|$ (norm of scalar) instead of $|g_{i}|$ used in the condition. Rewrite equation (3) as: $\xi_{i}(\bm{g},b)=\left\{\begin{array}[]{ll}(l+1)/b&\text{with probability }(b\cdot|g_{i}|)/\|\bm{g}\|_{2}-l\\
l/b&\text{otherwise.}\end{array}\right.$ to maintain consistent notation.

comments.title: Undefined quantization error notation
comments.quote: where $Q_{b}(\hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{k-1})$ is the last quantized upload from client $m$, $\varepsilon_{b}(\hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{k-1})$ and $\varepsilon_{b}(\bm{g}_{m}^{k})$ denote quantization errors, and $\{\xi_{d}\}_{d=1}^{D}$ are some predetermined constant weights.
comments.explanation: The notation $\varepsilon_{b}(\cdot)$ for quantization error is used without definition. Add the definition: $\varepsilon_{b}(\bm{g}) = \bm{g} - Q_{b}(\bm{g})$ to clarify that it represents the difference between the original gradient and its quantized version.

comments.title: Ambiguous gradient notation in lazy aggregation
comments.quote: Besides, $\hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{k}$ represents the actual gradient for aggregation from client $m$, which is $\bm{g}_{m}^{k}$ for $m\in\mathbb{M}\setminus\mathbb{M}_{0}^{k}$, while $\hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{k-1}$ for $m\in\mathbb{M}_{0}^{k}$.
comments.explanation: The notation is inconsistent: $\hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{k}$ is defined as the actual gradient for aggregation, but for clients in $\mathbb{M}_{0}^{k}$, it should be $\hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{k} = \hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{k-1}$, not $\hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{k-1}$ alone. Clarify that $\hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{k} = \begin{cases} \bm{g}_{m}^{k} & \text{if } m \in \mathbb{M} \setminus \mathbb{M}_{0}^{k} \\ \hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{k-1} & \text{if } m \in \mathbb{M}_{0}^{k} \end{cases}$.

comments.title: Missing justification for modified communication criterion
comments.quote: To better fit the larger quantization errors induced by fewer quantization bits in (7), we modify the communication criterion as follows to avoid the potential expansion of clients group to be skipped:
comments.explanation: The paper states that equation (8) is modified to "avoid the potential expansion of clients group to be skipped" but provides no mathematical justification for how the specific form of (8) achieves this goal. The relationship between the additional term $2\left\|\varepsilon_{b_{m}^{k}}(\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{k})-\varepsilon_{\hat{b}_{m}^{k-1}}(\hat{\boldsymbol{g}}_{m}^{k-1})\right\|_{2}^{2}$ and preventing client group expansion is unclear. Add a brief explanation: "The additional term $2\left\|\varepsilon_{b_{m}^{k}}(\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{k})-\varepsilon_{\hat{b}_{m}^{k-1}}(\hat{\boldsymbol{g}}_{m}^{k-1})\right\|_{2}^{2}$ accounts for the increased quantization variance when using fewer bits, making the communication threshold more conservative and thus preventing excessive client skipping."

comments.title: Inconsistent heterogeneous model complexity description
comments.quote: In this paper, we choose three various model complexity levels $r=\{a,b,c\}=\{1,0.5,0.25\}$.
comments.explanation: The text defines three complexity levels as $r=\{a,b,c\}=\{1,0.5,0.25\}$ but then refers to experiments with "100%-50% heterogeneous model architecture" and "100%-25% heterogeneous model architecture". This creates confusion about whether the experiments use two levels (50% and 25%) or three levels (100%, 50%, 25%). The notation $r=\{a,b,c\}$ suggests three distinct levels, but the experimental description only mentions two combinations. This inconsistency makes it difficult to understand the exact experimental setup.

comments.title: Ambiguous quantization level comparison for LAQ
comments.quote: For the choice of quantization level for fixed-bit LAQ, since AQUILA’s initial level $b_{0}$ is 2 for MNIST and 6 for CIFAR10, we compare AQUILA with LAQ-2 and LAQ-6 for MNIST and CIFAR 10 respectively. Besides, the performance of LAQ with the upperbound $b_{max}=16$ is also evaluated.
comments.explanation: The comparison methodology is unclear. AQUILA uses adaptive quantization starting from $b_0$ and can increase up to $b_{max}=16$, but the paper only compares against LAQ with fixed levels (LAQ-2, LAQ-6) and LAQ with the upperbound. This creates an unfair comparison since LAQ-16 uses a constant high quantization level while AQUILA starts low and adapts. A proper comparison should include LAQ with adaptive quantization but without the lazy aggregation component to isolate the contribution of each mechanism.

comments.title: Missing baseline methods in Fig. 4 explanation
comments.quote: Methods without adaptive quantization and lazy aggregation like FedAvg and QSGD are not included in Fig. 4 for simplicity.
comments.explanation: The exclusion of FedAvg and QSGD from Fig. 4 is problematic because these are fundamental baselines for communication efficiency. The figure shows "transmitted bits vs steps curves" but omits the most basic methods, making it impossible to assess whether AQUILA's adaptive quantization actually reduces communication compared to standard approaches. Since the paper claims communication efficiency, including these baselines in the bits-per-step comparison is essential, not optional.

comments.title: Inconsistent percentage reduction calculations
comments.quote: For instance, AQUILA saves  $65.98\%$  of transmitted bits compared with AdaQuantFL and  $62.05\%$  compared with the naive combination of LAQ and AdaQuantFL in the IID scenario with CIFAR10 dataset as shown in Fig. 3d, and other figures all show an obvious reduction in terms of the total transmitted bits required for convergence. Fig. 4 verifies that the quantization level selected by AQUILA will not continuously increase during training like AdaQuantFL and LAQ with AdaQuantFL.
comments.explanation: The percentage reduction claims appear inconsistent. If AQUILA saves 65.98% compared to AdaQuantFL and 62.05% compared to the naive combination, then the naive combination should use fewer bits than AdaQuantFL (since AQUILA saves less compared to it). However, the naive combination of LAQ and AdaQuantFL should logically use more bits than AdaQuantFL alone, as it adds LAQ's communication overhead. The authors should verify these calculations and ensure the baseline comparisons are correctly ordered.

comments.title: Overstated convergence guarantee claim
comments.quote: the proposed AQUILA has been proven to be capable of reducing the transmitted bits while maintaining the same convergence property and model performance compared against existing methods both theoretically and experimentally.
comments.explanation: The claim overstates what the paper actually demonstrates. Theorem 2 only shows convergence under specific learning rate conditions and Assumptions 1-2, but doesn't prove that AQUILA maintains the "same convergence property" as existing methods. The theorem establishes convergence but not equivalence of convergence rates. Similarly, the experiments show comparable performance but not identical performance. The claim should be weakened to reflect that AQUILA achieves convergence with reduced communication, not necessarily identical convergence properties.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated compatibility claim
comments.quote: The evaluation with Non-IID data distribution and various heterogeneous model architectures demonstrates that AQUILA is compatible to existing FL settings.
comments.explanation: This compatibility claim is not fully supported by the evidence presented. The paper only evaluates on 10 clients with mild non-IID distribution (2 classes per client), which doesn't demonstrate compatibility with the extreme heterogeneity common in real FL settings. "Various heterogeneous model architectures" is also vague - the paper should specify which architectures were tested and how they differ. The claim should be qualified to reflect the limited scope of the evaluation.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes AQUILA, a novel framework that jointly optimizes communication frequency and quantization level in federated learning by combining lazy aggregation with adaptive quantization. The idea of synergistically adjusting these two interdependent factors is well-motivated and addresses a real bottleneck in FL systems. The method is clearly presented, and the empirical results demonstrate significant communication savings across multiple settings, including non-IID data and heterogeneous models. The comparison to strong baselines and inclusion of ablation-style analysis add credibility to the claims.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical justification of the gradient loss bound lacks clarity and derivation details: The derivation of Theorem 1, which provides the upper bound on expected gradient loss, is referenced to the appendix but not included in the main paper or provided in the submission. Without access to the full derivation, it is difficult to verify whether the bound in equation (9) correctly accounts for both quantization error and lazy aggregation effects, especially under heterogeneous clients and adaptive bit selection. The expression involves terms dependent on $B_m$, the cumulative bits sent, which itself depends on the adaptive process—yet this dependency is not modeled in the bound. A more rigorous treatment would require a recursive or probabilistic analysis of how past quantization decisions affect current error. The claim that minimizing this bound leads to (7) via (10) needs explicit derivation, as the transition from (9) to (10) involves differentiating a sum over clients with respect to client-specific $b_m^k$, which may not be valid without further assumptions on independence.

- Adaptive quantization criterion (7) depends on gradient differences that may be unavailable or noisy in practice: Equation (7) defines the optimal quantization level using $\left\|\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{k}-\hat{\boldsymbol{g}}_{m}^{k-1}\right\|_{2}^{2}$, the squared norm of the difference between current and last uploaded gradient. However, in lazy aggregation, the client does not compute or store the full gradient at every round—only when it decides to transmit. This creates a circular dependency: the client needs the current gradient to decide the quantization level, but computing the full gradient incurs computation cost that the method aims to avoid. The paper does not specify how this gradient difference is estimated efficiently. If the client must compute the full gradient at every iteration just for this purpose, the computational savings are undermined, especially on resource-constrained devices. A discussion or alternative mechanism—such as estimating gradient change via local model updates or stochastic probing—is missing.

- Convergence analysis is superficial and does not establish new guarantees: The paper claims convergence by citing prior work on LAQ and stating that the Lyapunov function descends. However, AQUILA introduces time-varying, client-specific quantization levels and modifies the lazy aggregation condition (8), which invalidate direct application of previous convergence proofs. The analysis in Section 3.2 asserts that $\|\nabla f(\bm{\theta}^{k})\|_{2}^{2}$ converges based on $L$-smoothness, but this relies on the descent of $f(\bm{\theta}^{k}) - f(\bm{\theta}^{*})$, which itself depends on the quality of the aggregated gradient under adaptive quantization and skipping. No new convergence rate or error bound is provided for AQUILA. Theorem 2 compares bit usage to AdaQuantFL but does not prove convergence under AQUILA’s adaptive scheme—it only shows a numerical inequality under specific conditions. A proper convergence analysis should bound the expected gradient norm in terms of communication rounds, accounting for quantization variance and skipped updates.

- Empirical evaluation omits key baselines and ablation of core components: The experiments compare AQUILA to FedAvg, QSGD, fixed-bit LAQ, AdaQuantFL, and the naive combination of LAQ+AdaQuantFL, but do not include other state-of-the-art methods like Deep Gradient Compression (DGC) or FedPAQ, which are standard in communication-efficient FL. More importantly, there is no ablation study isolating the impact of adaptive quantization versus modified lazy aggregation. For instance, what happens if the original LAQ condition (4) is used with the new quantization rule (7)? Or if fixed quantization is used with the modified condition (8)? Without such breakdowns, it is unclear which component drives the improvement. Additionally, the claim of '50% reduction' is not consistently supported across figures—e.g., in Fig. 3a, AQUILA appears to converge with more bits than LAQ-2, contradicting the stated gains.

- Quantization level adaptation may not be feasible under strict client heterogeneity: The framework assumes that clients can dynamically adjust their quantization level per round, which requires coordination with the server on bit-width interpretation and possibly variable-length encoding. In heterogeneous hardware settings, some clients may lack support for variable bit-width arithmetic or efficient decoding on the server side. The paper does not discuss implementation complexity or overheads associated with signaling the chosen $b_m^k$ to the server. While equation (7) is client-local, the server must know $b_m^k$ to properly decode $Q_{b_m^k}(\bm{g}_m^k)$. The current quantizer definition (2) assumes fixed $b$, so extending it to variable $b$ per client per round requires clarification. This limits the practical deployability of the method in real-world FL systems.

- The adaptive criterion (7) risks instability when gradient differences are small or zero: The quantization level in (7) scales inversely with $\left\|\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{k}-\hat{\boldsymbol{g}}_{m}^{k-1}\right\|_{2}^{2}$. When this difference is very small—common in later training stages or flat loss regions—the denominator approaches zero, causing $b_m^k$ to spike unless clamped by $b_{max}$. While $b_{max}=16$ is used, this cap is arbitrary and not justified. A large jump in quantization level could cause a client to suddenly transmit high-precision gradients, increasing communication cost and potentially triggering more frequent transmissions due to the modified LAQ condition (8), counteracting the goal of efficiency. The paper does not analyze or report the frequency of such events, nor does it propose smoothing or regularization to stabilize bit selection.

- No worked example showing adaptive quantization in action: The paper proposes a novel adaptive quantization criterion (7) that depends on gradient differences, but does not compute it for any concrete model or data distribution. Readers need to see how (b_m^k)^* evolves over time in a simple, fully-specified setting—such as linear regression on synthetic data with known gradient dynamics—to build intuition for when and why it reduces bits compared to baselines. Without such a parametric example, the adaptive mechanism remains abstract and its stability near convergence is not grounded in observable behavior.

- Missing ablation of lazy aggregation vs quantization contributions: The experiments show AQUILA outperforms baselines but do not isolate whether gains come from the new quantization rule (7), the modified LAQ condition (8), or their interaction. A proper ablation would compare AQUILA against a version with fixed quantization and only (8), and another with fixed LAQ condition but (7). This is standard in systems papers to attribute performance gains. Without it, the claim of 'synergistic optimization' is asserted but not demonstrated.

- No simulation demonstrating non-vacuous improvement over AdaQuantFL: The paper claims AQUILA uses fewer bits than AdaQuantFL under the same convergence properties, but Theorem 2 only shows an inequality under specific learning rates and smoothness conditions—it does not show a concrete gap. A simulation with a small neural net on MNIST where both methods are run and the cumulative bit count and loss trajectory are plotted would verify the theory has bite. This is expected for any method claiming efficiency gains via adaptive control.

- No discussion of how to estimate gradient differences efficiently: The adaptive rule (7) requires computing the full local gradient at every round to evaluate ||g_m^k - ĝ_m^{k-1}||^2, which defeats the purpose of lazy aggregation if done unconditionally. The paper does not propose or evaluate a low-cost estimator—such as using model delta norms or stochastic probing—that would make this practical on resource-constrained devices. Given FL's focus on efficiency, this implementation gap undermines the method's deployability and needs explicit treatment.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a complete and self-contained derivation of Theorem 1 in the main paper or supplementary material, clarifying how the bound accounts for adaptive quantization and cumulative bit usage.

- Clarify how the gradient difference in equation (7) is computed without full gradient evaluation at every round, or propose an efficient approximation to maintain computational efficiency.

- Strengthen the convergence analysis by proving a new convergence rate or error bound for AQUILA under its joint adaptive quantization and lazy aggregation scheme, rather than relying solely on prior work.

- Include an ablation study that isolates the contribution of adaptive quantization (7) and modified lazy aggregation (8), and add comparisons to additional baselines like DGC or FedPAQ.

- Discuss the practical feasibility of variable-bit quantization, including signaling overhead, decoding complexity, and behavior under extreme gradient differences, with potential stabilization mechanisms.

comments.title: Gradient difference norm in (7) causes uncontrolled bit growth
comments.quote: $(b_{m}^{k})^{*}=b_{0}\cdot\sqrt{\left\|\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{1}-\hat{\boldsymbol{g}}_{m}^{0}\right\|_{2}^{2}/\left\|\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{k}-\hat{\boldsymbol{g}}_{m}^{k-1}\right\|_{2}^{2}}.$ (7)
comments.explanation: Equation (7) uses a fixed numerator based on the first-round gradient difference, while the denominator decays as training progresses. When the current gradient change becomes small—common in later stages—the ratio diverges, causing $(b_m^k)^*$ to spike unless clamped by $b_{\max}$. The paper relies on $b_{\max}=16$ to prevent overflow, but this cap is arbitrary and not justified. The claim that this design prevents 'tremendous' increases in quantization level contradicts its own functional form. A stable rule should compare current changes to recent history, not a single initial value. Consider replacing the static baseline with a moving average or recent gradient difference to avoid path dependence on early noisy updates.

comments.title: Quantization error term in (8) is uncomputable in practice
comments.quote: $\left\|Q_{b_{m}^{k}}(\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{k})-Q_{\hat{b}_{m}^{k-1}}(\hat{\boldsymbol{g}}_{m}^{k-1})\right\|_{2}^{2}\geq\frac{\sum_{d=1}^{D}\xi_{d}\left\|\boldsymbol{\theta}^{k+1-d}-\boldsymbol{\theta}^{k-d}\right\|_{2}^{2}}{\alpha^{2}M^{2}}+2\left\|\varepsilon_{b_{m}^{k}}(\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{k})-\varepsilon_{\hat{b}_{m}^{k-1}}(\hat{\boldsymbol{g}}_{m}^{k-1})\right\|_{2}^{2},$ (8)
comments.explanation: The term $\left\|\varepsilon_{b_{m}^{k}}(\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{k}) - \varepsilon_{\hat{b}_{m}^{k-1}}(\hat{\boldsymbol{g}}_{m}^{k-1})\right\|_{2}^{2}$ depends on full-precision gradients $\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{k}$ and $\hat{\boldsymbol{g}}_{m}^{k-1}$, which are not stored after quantization in typical FL systems. Clients only retain or transmit quantized values, making the quantization error $\varepsilon_b(\boldsymbol{g}) = Q_b(\boldsymbol{g}) - \boldsymbol{g}$ unobservable. Even if gradients were stored, the norm of the difference between errors from different bit-widths lacks a clear interpretation due to mismatched representations. This renders (8) non-implementable as written. Replace the error difference with a bound based on known variance, such as $\mathbb{E}[\|\varepsilon_b\|^2] \leq c \cdot 2^{-b} \|\boldsymbol{g}\|^2$, which can be estimated from available quantities.

comments.title: Derivation of (10) from (9) ignores non-differentiability and recursive dependence
comments.quote:
For each client $m$, the optimal $b_{m}^{k}$ is selected by setting the first derivative of the upper bound (9) to zero:

$(b_{m}^{k})^{*}=\sqrt{\left(2\sigma^{2}B_{m}\log_{e}(2)\right)/\left(d_{m}\left\lVert\bm{g}_{m}^{k}-\hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{k-1}\right\rVert_{2}^{2}\right)}.$ (10)
comments.explanation: The transition from (9) to (10) is mathematically invalid. First, (9) contains $\lceil \log_2(4b_m^k) \rceil$, a piecewise constant function that is non-differentiable almost everywhere. Standard calculus cannot be applied to minimize it via derivative. Second, $B_m$—the cumulative bits sent—is treated as fixed, but it depends on all prior $b_m^j$, creating a recursive dependence. Minimizing (9) with respect to $b_m^k$ affects future $B_m$, yet this feedback is ignored. Third, the appearance of $\log_e(2)$ in (10) suggests differentiation of $\log_2(b)$, but this term is inside a ceiling function. The derivation lacks justification and cannot be recovered without a proper treatment of discrete bit-length effects or dynamic bit accumulation.

comments.title: Variance term in (9) misattributes quantization noise from past rounds
comments.quote: \frac{3 \alpha^{2}}{M} \sum_{m \in \mathbb{M}}\left(\frac{d \sigma^{2}}{(\hat{b}_{m}^{k-1})^{2}}+\frac{d \sigma^{2}}{(b_{m}^{k})^{2}}\right)+\frac{3
comments.explanation: The bound (9) includes $\frac{d \sigma^{2}}{(\hat{b}_{m}^{k-1})^{2}}$, implying that the quantization noise from a previous round contributes to current gradient error. However, if client $m$ transmits at round $k$, the old quantization level $\hat{b}_m^{k-1}$ is irrelevant—the new gradient $\boldsymbol{g}_m^k$ is quantized at $b_m^k$, and the server discards the old value. If the client skips, no new quantization occurs, so there is no additional noise from $b_m^k$. The current form double-counts or misattributes noise by summing over both levels. The correct variance contribution should depend only on whether a new gradient is sent: $\frac{d \sigma^{2}}{(b_m^k)^2}$ if transmitted, 0 otherwise. The current expression misrepresents the error dynamics and weakens the theoretical foundation.

comments.title: Theorem 2's inequality chain lacks justification and conflates client and global quantities
comments.quote: $(b_{m}^{k})^{*}=b_{0}\sqrt{\frac{\left\lVert\bm{g}_{m}^{1}-\hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{0}\right\rVert_{2}^{2}}{\left\lVert\bm{g}_{m}^{k}-\hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{k-1}\right\rVert_{2}^{2}}}\leq b_{0}\sqrt{\frac{\alpha^{2}M^{2}L^{3}\left\lVert\bm{\theta}^{1}-\bm{\theta}^{0}\right\rVert_{2}^{2}}{2\xi[f(\bm{\theta}^{k})-f(\bm{\theta}^{*})]}}\leq b_{0}\sqrt{\frac{f(\bm{\theta}^{0})-f(\bm{\theta}^{*})}{f(\bm{\theta}^{k})-f(\bm{\theta}^{*})}},$ (11)
comments.explanation: The chain of inequalities in (11) is unjustified. The first term is client-specific and based on gradient differences, while the last term depends on global loss values. There is no derivation linking $\|\boldsymbol{g}_m^1 - \hat{\boldsymbol{g}}_m^0\|^2 / \|\boldsymbol{g}_m^k - \hat{\boldsymbol{g}}_m^{k-1}\|^2$ to $f(\bm{\theta}^k) - f(\bm{\theta}^*)$. Although $L$-smoothness gives $\|\nabla f(\bm{\theta}^k)\|^2 \leq 2L[f(\bm{\theta}^k) - f(\bm{\theta}^*)]$, this applies to the global gradient, not individual client updates. The paper provides no argument that the client gradient ratio is bounded by the global objective residual. As a result, the conclusion that AQUILA uses fewer bits than AdaQuantFL is asserted, not proven. A valid comparison would require bounding client-level quantities in terms of global convergence, which is missing.

comments.title: Convergence claim relies on unverified Lyapunov descent under modified dynamics
comments.quote: Convergence guarantee of federated learning with lazy aggregation has been well discussed in *Sun et al. (2020)*. More specifically, both the objective residual $f(\bm{\theta}^{k})-f(\bm{\theta}^{*})$ and the parameter differences term in Lyapunov function are guaranteed to descend along with the training process.
comments.explanation: The paper cites Sun et al. (2020) for convergence, but AQUILA introduces client-specific adaptive quantization and a modified lazy aggregation condition (8), which alter the update dynamics. The Lyapunov function (12) assumes the same error bounds and descent properties as LAQ, but the variable bit-widths and revised threshold invalidate direct application. The claim that $f(\bm{\theta}^k) - f(\bm{\theta}^*)$ descends is not proven under AQUILA’s updates. In particular, the adaptive quantization and skipped gradients introduce time-varying bias and variance that may disrupt descent. The inequality $\|\nabla f(\bm{\theta}^k)\|^2 \leq 2L[f(\bm{\theta}^k) - f(\bm{\theta}^*)]$ holds, but this does not imply convergence unless the objective decreases in expectation. Without a proof of sufficient descent, the convergence claim is unsubstantiated.

comments.title: Algorithm requires full gradient computation at every round, undermining efficiency
comments.quote: Each client $m$ computes $\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{k}$ with local training data, and then uses it to select an optimal quantization level by (7).
comments.explanation: The adaptive rule (7) requires the full local gradient $\boldsymbol{g}_m^k$ to compute $\|\boldsymbol{g}_m^k - \hat{\boldsymbol{g}}_m^{k-1}\|^2$. However, lazy aggregation is designed to reduce computation by skipping updates when changes are small. If the client must compute the full gradient at every round just to decide the quantization level, the computational savings of lazy aggregation are undermined—especially on resource-constrained devices. The paper does not propose a low-cost estimator (e.g., based on model delta or stochastic probing) to approximate the gradient change. This creates a practical implementation gap: the method may reduce communication at the cost of increased local computation, which is not accounted for in the efficiency analysis.

comments.title: Global model difference term in (8) may not be available client-side
comments.quote: $\left\|Q_{b_{m}^{k}}(\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{k})-Q_{\hat{b}_{m}^{k-1}}(\hat{\boldsymbol{g}}_{m}^{k-1})\right\|_{2}^{2}\geq\frac{\sum_{d=1}^{D}\xi_{d}\left\|\boldsymbol{\theta}^{k+1-d}-\boldsymbol{\theta}^{k-d}\right\|_{2}^{2}}{\alpha^{2}M^{2}}+2\left\|\varepsilon_{b_{m}^{k}}(\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{k})-\varepsilon_{\hat{b}_{m}^{k-1}}(\hat{\boldsymbol{g}}_{m}^{k-1})\right\|_{2}^{2},$ (8)
comments.explanation: The threshold in (8) includes $\sum_{d=1}^{D} \xi_d \|\boldsymbol{\theta}^{k+1-d} - \boldsymbol{\theta}^{k-d}\|^2$, which depends on historical global models. Clients typically do not store past global models beyond the current $\boldsymbol{\theta}^k$, especially under memory constraints. Without access to $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{k-d}$ for $d \geq 1$, clients cannot compute this sum locally. The paper does not specify whether the server broadcasts this term or precomputes it. If not, the condition cannot be evaluated on-device, breaking decentralized execution. Clarify the coordination mechanism: either have the server broadcast the threshold baseline or redefine the condition to use only locally available information.

comments.title: Claimed 42.6% bit reduction in MNIST contradicted by Figure 3a
comments.quote: For example, Fig. 3a indicates that AQUILA reduces  $42.6\%$  of total transmitted bits compared with LAQ-2 and  $92.32\%$  compared with LAQ-16 in MNIST.
comments.explanation: Figure 3a shows that AQUILA converges at approximately 1.8e6 bits, while LAQ-2 converges at around 1.6e6 bits—indicating LAQ-2 uses fewer bits than AQUILA. This directly contradicts the claim of a 42.6% reduction. Either the percentage is miscalculated or misattributed. If AQUILA uses more bits than a baseline, it cannot be said to reduce transmission by a positive percentage. This error undermines the paper's quantitative efficiency claims. The reported numbers must reflect the actual bit counts in the figure, or the figure must be corrected to match the stated results.

comments.title: LAQ-2 vs AQUILA comparison uses unfair fixed-bit baseline
comments.quote: For the choice of quantization level for fixed-bit LAQ, since AQUILA’s initial level $b_{0}$ is 2 for MNIST and 6 for CIFAR10, we compare AQUILA with LAQ-2 and LAQ-6 for MNIST and CIFAR 10 respectively.
comments.explanation: The comparison is biased: AQUILA starts at $b_0=2$ but can adaptively increase up to $b_{\max}=16$, while LAQ-2 is fixed at 2 bits. This pits a dynamic, high-capability method against a static, low-bit regime that will naturally transmit more frequently under lazy aggregation. A fair comparison would evaluate fixed-bit LAQ across multiple bit-widths (e.g., 2, 6, 10, 16) to show that AQUILA’s adaptive strategy outperforms all fixed settings. As written, the design choice conflates the benefit of adaptation with a suboptimal baseline, overstating AQUILA’s gains.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a federated learning framework that combines adaptive quantization with lazy aggregation to reduce communication costs. The core idea of jointly optimizing quantization levels and communication frequency is timely and practically motivated. However, the paper suffers from several significant issues in its theoretical claims, empirical validation, and framing that must be addressed before it meets the standards of a top venue.

Overview issues:

- Convergence proof is incomplete and relies on unverified assumptions: The convergence analysis in Section 3.2 is presented as a straightforward extension of existing LAQ and AdaQuantFL results, but the paper does not provide a complete convergence proof for AQUILA. Theorem 1 bounds the expected gradient loss from lazy aggregation, but this bound depends on unknown constants (d_m, σ²) and does not directly lead to a convergence rate for the global objective. Theorem 2 compares AQUILA's quantization level to AdaQuantFL's under a restrictive learning rate condition, but does not establish that the AQUILA update rule converges at all. The paper states that convergence is "guaranteed" by referencing LAQ's Lyapunov function, yet AQUILA's adaptive quantization changes the dynamics in ways that break the original LAQ proof. The authors need to provide a self-contained convergence analysis that accounts for the interaction between adaptive quantization and lazy aggregation, including a clear convergence rate (e.g., O(1/T) or O(1/√T)) under standard assumptions.

- Empirical evaluation is insufficient to support the claimed benefits: The experiments are limited to MNIST and CIFAR-10 with only 10 clients. This is far from the realistic FL settings (100+ clients, more complex datasets like FEMNIST or StackOverflow) that the field expects. The paper claims AQUILA reduces transmitted bits by 50% or more, but these claims are based on a single training run per setting—no error bars or multiple seeds are reported. The comparison baselines are also problematic: FedAvg and QSGD are included but without any communication compression, making the comparison unfair. The paper does not report test accuracy, only training loss, which is insufficient to demonstrate that model quality is maintained. An ablation study separating the effects of adaptive quantization vs. lazy aggregation is missing. The authors should run experiments with at least 3-5 seeds, report test accuracy with standard deviations, include more realistic datasets and client counts, and provide an ablation study.

- Communication cost accounting is incomplete and potentially misleading: The paper reports total transmitted bits but does not account for the overhead of quantization metadata (e.g., the norm ∥g∥₂, sign bits, and the quantization level indices) in the reported savings. The formula C_b = d⌈log₂(b+1)⌉ + d + 32 is used for a single quantized gradient, but the paper does not clarify whether this overhead is included in the total bits reported in Figures 3 and 4. Additionally, the lazy aggregation mechanism requires clients to send a flag indicating whether they are skipping, and the server must broadcast the global model each round—these control signals are not accounted for. The claimed savings may be significantly overestimated. The authors should provide a detailed breakdown of communication costs, including all metadata and control overhead, and report the total bytes transmitted end-to-end.

- The adaptive quantization criterion lacks theoretical justification for its claimed optimality: Equation (7) is derived by setting the derivative of the upper bound in Theorem 1 to zero, but this derivation assumes that the terms involving σ² and d_m are constant and that the bound is tight. The resulting criterion (b_m^k)* = b_0 · √(∥g_m^1 - ĝ_m^0∥₂² / ∥g_m^k - ĝ_m^{k-1}∥₂²) is then simplified by dividing by b_m^1, but this step implicitly assumes that the unknown constants cancel out, which is not justified. Moreover, the paper does not verify that this choice actually minimizes the gradient loss in practice—it only minimizes an upper bound that may be loose. The authors should either provide a rigorous justification for the optimality of (7) under the stated assumptions, or present empirical evidence that the criterion is near-optimal compared to a grid search over quantization levels.

- The lazy aggregation condition (8) is not properly derived or validated: Equation (8) modifies the original LAQ condition (4) to accommodate heterogeneous quantization levels, but the derivation is relegated to the appendix with no details provided in the main text. The condition includes a term 2∥ε_{b_m^k}(g_m^k) - ε_{b_{m}^{k-1}}(ĝ_m^{k-1})∥₂² that depends on quantization errors from two different quantization levels. The paper does not explain how this term is computed in practice (quantization errors are typically random and not directly observable), nor does it analyze how this modification affects the convergence guarantees. The authors should provide a clear derivation of (8) in the main text, explain how the quantization error difference is estimated or bounded, and demonstrate that the modified condition still ensures convergence.

- Critical implementation details and hyperparameter sensitivity are missing: The paper does not report how the initial quantization level b_0 is chosen for different tasks (b_0=2 for MNIST, b_0=6 for CIFAR-10) or how sensitive the results are to this choice. The maximum quantization level b_max=16 is used without justification. The learning rate α and the LAQ parameters (ξ_d, D) are taken from prior work, but no sensitivity analysis is provided. Given that the method has multiple interacting hyperparameters (b_0, b_max, α, ξ_d, D), the paper should include a sensitivity study showing how performance varies with these choices. Without this, it is unclear whether the reported results are robust or cherry-picked.

- No test accuracy reported: The paper reports only training loss versus total transmitted bits, but does not report test accuracy for any experiment. In federated learning, the key metric is whether the model maintains predictive performance while reducing communication. Without test accuracy, readers cannot assess whether AQUILA preserves model quality. The authors should add plots or tables of test accuracy (with standard deviations across multiple seeds) for all settings in Figures 3 and 4, and compare these to baselines at equivalent communication budgets.

- No ablation study isolating adaptive quantization from lazy aggregation: AQUILA combines two mechanisms: adaptive quantization (criterion (7)) and lazy aggregation (condition (8)). The paper claims these are synergistic, but provides no ablation study that isolates the contribution of each component. Without such an experiment, it is unclear whether the gains come from the adaptive quantization rule, the modified lazy aggregation condition, or their interaction. The authors should run comparisons of (i) AQUILA full, (ii) AQUILA with fixed quantization (i.e., LAQ only), (iii) AQUILA with lazy aggregation disabled (i.e., adaptive quantization only), and (iv) the naive combination of AdaQuantFL+LAQ, on at least one dataset (e.g., CIFAR-10 Non-IID).

- Missing derivation of the modified lazy aggregation condition (8): Equation (8) modifies the original LAQ condition to accommodate heterogeneous quantization levels, but the derivation is not provided in the main text and is only referenced to the appendix. Moreover, the term involving the difference of quantization errors from two different levels is not explained: how is this term computed or bounded in practice? The paper should include a clear derivation of (8) in the main text, explain how the quantization error difference is estimated, and show that the modified condition still ensures convergence (or at least does not violate the original LAQ guarantees).

- No sensitivity analysis for key hyperparameters: The method has several interacting hyperparameters: initial quantization level b_0, maximum quantization level b_max, learning rate α, and the LAQ parameters ξ_d and D. The paper chooses b_0=2 for MNIST and b_0=6 for CIFAR-10, and b_max=16, without justification or sensitivity analysis. Given that the method's performance could depend heavily on these choices, the authors should include a sensitivity study varying b_0 (e.g., 2, 4, 8, 16) and b_max (e.g., 8, 16, 32) on at least one dataset, reporting both communication cost and test accuracy. This would demonstrate robustness and provide practical guidance.

Recommendation: Major revision. The paper addresses a relevant problem and proposes a reasonable approach, but the theoretical analysis is incomplete, the empirical validation is insufficient for the claims made, and several methodological details are missing. A revision must provide a complete convergence proof, more thorough experiments with proper statistical rigor, and a clear accounting of communication costs.

Revision targets:

- Provide a self-contained convergence analysis for AQUILA that accounts for the interaction between adaptive quantization and lazy aggregation, including a clear convergence rate under standard assumptions.

- Run experiments on at least two realistic FL benchmarks (e.g., FEMNIST, CIFAR-100) with 100+ clients, report test accuracy with standard deviations over 3-5 seeds, and include an ablation study separating the effects of adaptive quantization and lazy aggregation.

- Provide a detailed breakdown of communication costs including all metadata and control overhead, and report total bytes transmitted end-to-end rather than just gradient bits.

- Include a sensitivity analysis for key hyperparameters (b_0, b_max, learning rate) and justify the chosen values.

- Derive the modified lazy aggregation condition (8) in the main text and explain how the quantization error difference term is computed or bounded in practice.

comments.title: Equation (8) contains an unobservable quantization error difference term
comments.quote: $\left\|Q_{b_{m}^{k}}(\boldsymbol{\hat{g}}_{m}^{k})-Q_{\tilde{b}_{m}^{k-1}}(\tilde{\boldsymbol{g}}_{m}^{k-1})\right\|_{2}^{2}\geq\frac{\sum_{d=1}^{D}\xi_{d}\left\|\boldsymbol{\theta}^{k+1-d}-\boldsymbol{\theta}^{k-d}\right\|_{2}^{2}}{\alpha^{2}M^{2}}+2\left\|\varepsilon_{b_{m}^{k}}(\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{k})-\varepsilon_{\tilde{b}_{m}^{k-1}}(\tilde{\boldsymbol{g}}_{m}^{k-1})\right\|_{2}^{2},$ (8)
comments.explanation: The term $\|\varepsilon_{b_m^k}(g_m^k) - \varepsilon_{\tilde{b}_m^{k-1}}(\tilde{g}_m^{k-1})\|_2^2$ involves quantization errors $\varepsilon$, defined as the difference between the true gradient and its quantized version. In practice, the true gradient $g_m^k$ is not known to the client after quantization — only the quantized value $Q_{b_m^k}(g_m^k)$ is available. Therefore, the quantization error $\varepsilon_{b_m^k}(g_m^k)$ is not directly observable, making this term impossible to compute exactly. The paper does not explain how this term is estimated or bounded in practice. Without a practical method to evaluate this term, condition (8) cannot be implemented as stated. The authors should clarify how this term is computed or provide a surrogate that is observable.

comments.title: Optimal quantization derivation ignores non-differentiable ceiling
comments.quote:
For each client $m$, the optimal $b_{m}^{k}$ is selected by setting the first derivative of the upper bound (9) to zero:

$(b_{m}^{k})^{*}=\sqrt{\left(\left.2\sigma^{2}B_{m}log_{e}(2)\right)/\left(d_{m}\left\lVert\bm{g}_{m}^{k}-\hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{k-1}\right\rVert_{2}^{2}\right)\right)}.$
comments.explanation: The upper bound (9) contains the term $d\lceil\log_2(4b_m^k)\rceil / B_m$, which involves the ceiling function $\lceil\cdot\rceil$. The ceiling function is not differentiable, so taking a derivative with respect to $b_m^k$ is not mathematically valid. The derivation also treats $\hat{b}_m^{k-1}$ as constant when differentiating, but $\hat{b}_m^{k-1}$ may depend on $b_m^k$ through the adaptive process. The paper should either replace the ceiling with a differentiable approximation (e.g., $\log_2(4b_m^k)+1$) or justify why the ceiling can be ignored (e.g., by treating $b_m^k$ as continuous and noting that the ceiling is a step function that can be approximated). Provide a rigorous derivation of (10) that addresses the non-differentiability of the ceiling function.

comments.title: Convergence analysis does not account for adaptive quantization
comments.quote: Convergence guarantee of federated learning with lazy aggregation has been well discussed in *Sun et al. (2020)*. More specifically, both the objective residual $f(\bm{\theta}^{k})-f(\bm{\theta}^{*})$ and the parameter differences term in Lyapunov function are guaranteed to descend along with the training process. Therefore, the squared norm of the gradient $\nabla f(\bm{\theta}^{k})$ is also guaranteed to converge based on the $L$-smoothness assumption which results in: $\|\nabla f(\bm{\theta}^{k})\|_{2}^{2}\leq 2L[f(\bm{\theta}^{k})-f(\bm{\theta}^{*})]$.
comments.explanation: The Lyapunov function (12) does not incorporate any terms related to quantization levels or the adaptive selection criterion (7). The paper claims convergence by citing Sun et al. (2020) for lazy aggregation, but AQUILA's adaptive quantization introduces dynamics (e.g., varying $b_m^k$ and skipping decisions based on (8)) that are not present in the original LAQ framework. The inequality $\|\nabla f(\theta^k)\|_2^2 \leq 2L[f(\theta^k)-f(\theta^*)]$ is a standard consequence of $L$-smoothness for convex functions or under the Polyak-Łojasiewicz condition, but it does not hold generally for non-convex functions without additional assumptions. Even if the objective residual converges to zero, this inequality only provides an upper bound, not a guarantee that the gradient norm converges to zero. The paper needs a self-contained convergence analysis that accounts for the interaction between adaptive quantization and lazy aggregation, and that establishes a convergence rate for $\|\nabla f(\theta^k)\|_2^2$ under the stated assumptions.

comments.title: Equation (7) derivation assumes unknown constants cancel without justification
comments.quote:
To get the optimization target, we first derive an upperbound for the expected gradient loss in terms of quantization bits as elaborated in Section 3.2, which gives a novel adaptive quantization criterion (7) that selects the quantization level for client $m$ at iteration $k$ based on initial quantization level $b_{0}$ and the change between client $m$’s newly computed gradient $g_{m}^{k}$ and the last uploaded gradient $\tilde{g}_{m}^{k-1}$:

$(b_{m}^{k})^{*}=b_{0}\cdot\sqrt{\left\|\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{1}-\boldsymbol{\hat{g}}_{m}^{0}\right\|_{2}^{2}/\left\|\boldsymbol{g}_{m}^{k}-\boldsymbol{\hat{g}}_{m}^{k-1}\right\|_{2}^{2}}.$ (7)
comments.explanation: The derivation of (7) is claimed to come from minimizing an upper bound on expected gradient loss derived in Section 3.2. However, the paper does not show how the unknown constants (e.g., $d_m$, $\sigma^2$) from Theorem 1 cancel out to yield a criterion that depends only on the ratio of gradient norms. The step from the upper bound to (7) implicitly assumes these constants are identical across clients and iterations, which is not justified. Without a rigorous derivation showing that the minimizer of the bound is independent of these constants, the claimed optimality of (7) is unsupported. The authors should either provide the full derivation showing the cancellation, or acknowledge that (7) is a heuristic and justify it empirically.

comments.title: Theorem 2 inequality chain lacks derivation
comments.quote:
Under Assumption 1 and 2, if the learning rate $\alpha$ satisfies $\alpha\leq(1/ML)\cdot\sqrt{2P\xi/L}$, the following inequality holds:

$(b_{m}^{k})^{*}=b_{0}\sqrt{\frac{\left\lVert\bm{g}_{m}^{1}-\hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{0}\right\rVert_{2}^{2}}{\left\lVert\bm{g}_{m}^{k}-\hat{\bm{g}}_{m}^{k-1}\right\rVert_{2}^{2}}}\leq b_{0}\sqrt{\frac{\alpha^{2}M^{2}L^{3}\left\lVert\bm{\theta}^{1}-\bm{\theta}^{0}\right\rVert_{2}^{2}}{2\xi[f(\bm{\theta}^{k})-f(\bm{\theta}^{*})]}}\leq b_{0}\sqrt{\frac{f(\bm{\theta}^{0})-f(\bm{\theta}^{*})}{f(\bm{\theta}^{k})-f(\bm{\theta}^{*})}},$
comments.explanation: The first inequality introduces constants $P$ and $\xi$ without definition in the main text, and the condition $\alpha \leq (1/ML)\sqrt{2P\xi/L}$ does not appear in the inequality itself.
